name: Maya R.
body: I've been using Claude as a kind of pair-reader for dense philosophy texts. Not to summarize, but to push back on my reading. I'll explain what I think Heidegger is saying and ask it to argue the strongest counter-reading. Then I sit with both and write. The conversation makes my notes sharper.

name: Prof. Lin
body: What worries me less than I expected: students using AI to write. What worries me more: students using AI to skip the discomfort of not understanding something. The struggle is the learning. We have to be careful what we make easy.

name: Anonymous
body: Office hours hack: I paste the lecture transcript into an AI tool and have it generate a list of questions a confused student might ask. Then I walk into office hours with three real ones. My professor said my questions got noticeably better this term.

name: Devon K.
body: I keep thinking about what 'expertise' means now. The fact that I can ask a tool to draft a memo as well as I would have ten years ago doesn't make my judgment obsolete — it makes my judgment the whole job. That feels harder, not easier.

name: Sam W.
body: In my linguistics seminar a few years back, the professor banned AI outright. Now I wonder what that class would look like if she'd instead made us defend our prompts the same way we defend our citations. The interesting work is in the asking.
